title_en: Holed Up in the Castle, Immersed in AI and Cooking
excerpt_en: 7/3 July 3rd. Once again today, I spent an intense session with AI. If you asked me what I did, I can only say I spent the entire day immersed in digital creation, pushing AI to its limits. The one new thing worth mentioning is that I've recently
body_en:
7/3

July 3rd. Once again today, I spent an intense session with AI. If you asked me what I did, I can only say I spent the entire day immersed in digital creation, pushing AI to its limits. The one new thing worth mentioning is that I've recently started cooking seriously.

Cooking at the Castle: Simple Joys and Efficiency

When I say cooking, it's still simple stuff like boiling pasta or frying meat, but the frequency has increased dramatically compared to before.

Over the past year, I'd been living an address-hopper lifestyle, bouncing from hotel to hotel, sharing common spaces with lots of people and sometimes competing for kitchen space.

But life at the castle is different. Only myself and one other volunteer use the kitchen, so it's extremely comfortable. I can freely use the fridge too, so I've started buying vegetables in bulk and actively using onions, potatoes, garlic, and such to add color to my pasta.

I'm experimenting on my own, making meals that consider nutritional balance. Part of me wants to try more elaborate dishes, but right now I'm prioritizing getting quick nutrition over spending time and money on fancy cooking.

Life at the castle means almost no opportunities to eat out. First of all, it's a 3-kilometer one-way trip from the castle to the town center, nearly an hour on foot.

If I made that round trip every day, it would eat up an hour and a half to two hours. That's way too much wasted time. Especially now, since I'm focused on AI work, I want to stay near my computer as much as possible.

So cooking for myself becomes the only option. There are no opportunities to go out drinking with anyone or eat at restaurants, so basically I can focus on my work in solitude. For me, it's an incredibly grateful situation, and I'd say it's the perfect environment for diving deep into work.

Even while I'm cooking, AI keeps running in the background.

Accelerating AI Development and Visions for the Future

Currently, I'm mainly using a service called "Claude Code." I pay $100 per month (roughly 15,000 to 16,000 yen), and I'm developing web services on that platform. The focus is on building websites, and this AI development process is so fascinating that I've become completely absorbed in it.

Once I enter this state of focus, I don't feel like doing any other work, and I have no desire to go see anyone. For the rest of July, I plan to hole up in the castle and devote all my energy to web service development and improving my "Vibes Coding" skills.

Things that once could only be done by specialized engineers and programmers can now be accomplished by someone like me with zero programming knowledge, as long as I have images and ideas in my head. We've entered an era where you can make those ideas a reality.

This technology only appeared very recently, and I feel like I've caught this wave of evolution at the perfect timing. Contributing to the community through volunteer work while dedicating my spare time to leveling up my AI skills. This well-balanced lifestyle is a huge source of motivation for me.

The volunteer work isn't something with strict time requirements either. The owner's consideration in letting me freely work during gaps in my schedule supports this efficient way of working. Producing results in short volunteer sessions, then dedicating all remaining time to AI training. This style feels incredibly comfortable to me and gives me a real sense of fulfillment.

In fact, over the past few days of nonstop Vibes Coding, my skills have improved at an astonishing pace. Before, I could barely build one site on one screen, but now I can work on 3 or even 4 sites simultaneously.

In just 3 or 4 days, my work efficiency has tripled or quadrupled.

Website construction that would normally cost hundreds of thousands or even millions of yen can now be done with just my labor costs and minimal expenses like API fees and server costs.

Excluding my own labor costs, it's now possible for just tens of thousands of yen. I honestly can't hide my amazement.

Having an environment where nobody disturbs me and I can focus on work is also huge. I basically live a life of moving from hotel to hotel, and I often stay in dormitory-style shared rooms where there's always someone around.

But at the castle, I've been given my own private room, so I can really hunker down and concentrate on my work without anyone bothering me. There might not be a better place anywhere for AI learning and development.

In this environment, I want to continue learning and give my all to creating new value together with AI.

title_en: Digital Marketing Consultation and Taking on a Hackathon
excerpt_en: 3 Weeks of Castle Life: Digital Marketing Consultation and Taking on a Hackathon. July 4th. It's been about 3 weeks since I started living in the castle. Every day, I've been steadily working on volunteer tasks like cleaning and maintaining the castle at my own pace, but
body_en:
3 Weeks of Castle Life: Digital Marketing Consultation and Taking on a Hackathon

July 4th. It's been about 3 weeks since I started living in the castle. Every day, I've been steadily working on volunteer tasks like cleaning and maintaining the castle at my own pace, but today something a little special happened in the afternoon.

An Unexpected Offer from the Castle Manager

The castle's representative, who also serves as my sponsor, asked if I'd like to have a meeting.

Wondering what it could be about, I headed over, and it turned out to be a consultation about whether I could help with digital marketing. I think he knew that I have some knowledge in that area, since I work as a photographer and run my own company and brand.

Of course, the castle manager is also a business owner who has been running things in a more traditional way, but he said he was interested in digital marketing. Along the way, he told me about the various projects he's working on.

After the meeting ended, I received an email with an offer to participate in a new project.

That project was the **"Climathon."

"Climathon": Tackling Global Challenges from the Local Level

Apparently, a Climathon is a competitive hackathon where communities tackle global climate change challenges from the local level. Basically, it's an event where people compete with ideas about climate change.

This is set to be held in Ireland in October of this year, and as it turns out, the organizer is none other than the castle manager himself. I never expected something like this to come my way.

Honestly, I don't have a particularly strong interest in the theme of "a competitive hackathon to tackle global climate challenges from the local level" at this point. But since it's such a generous invitation, I'm thinking about developing an interest in it and putting together a presentation while doing my volunteer work at the castle. He really wanted me to participate, and I generally don't turn down requests from the castle owner, so I've decided to go for it.

A Breakthrough into Ireland's Business Community

If I hadn't been living in this castle, an opportunity like this probably never would have come my way. I wouldn't have even known such an event existed in the first place.

It's been 11 months since I came to Ireland, and from my experience so far, I'd felt that very few Japanese business owners or Japanese people in general have managed to deeply integrate into the kind of communities where local Irish companies and business owners participate.

This Climathon participation might just be a huge chance to break into Ireland's deep network of business owners. That's exactly why I want to take the plunge and go for it.

Over the past 11 months or so, I've tried various approaches through conventional means. I attended whiskey industry events and Japan-Ireland cultural exchange events like "Japan Experience," but in my case, I just couldn't get accepted into the Irish business community.

I felt like there was an invisible wall among the Irish people. I also painfully realized that just living in Ireland doesn't automatically lead to business opportunities, and looking at other Japanese people living in Ireland, very few seemed to have deeply penetrated that community. I also almost never saw Japanese business owners collaborating with Irish business owners.

That's why I believe it's absolutely essential for me to find my own way into Ireland's business world. Flip that around, and if I can clear this hurdle, I might become the kind of person where people say "we need to get MaSU on the project team"** when they want to do business in Ireland.

I want to establish a position where people say "you should consult with MaSU" when it comes to doing business in Ireland. Since I'm going to be staying in Ireland for a full year, I have goals like that too. Right now, my main focus is on firmly rooting myself in the community and contributing.

My First Hackathon: Venturing into the Unknown

So that's how all sorts of new opportunities have been coming in. I definitely want to participate in this Climathon event.

When I asked the owner, he said there's no specific deadline for registration, but there will be presentations in October. I still don't know the details of whether I'll be presenting or just need to enter, but if I can put together a good presentation, there might be an opportunity to present on-site.

For now, I'll register and participate. I might be able to make use of the castle's facilities, and including my own activities, I'd like to contribute something toward solving challenges related to climate change from the local level.

It's only been 3 weeks since I started living in the castle, but I've already received a concrete offer like this. If the relationship between the castle owner and me hadn't been going well, an opportunity like this would never have come my way. I'm truly grateful.

This is my first hackathon challenge, but I'm going to research and prepare from here on out.

excerpt_en: 7/24 Hey everyone! July 24th, and my days in Ireland are finally entering the home stretch. It was a jam-packed day.
body_en:
7/24

Hey everyone!

July 24th, and my days in Ireland are finally entering the home stretch. It was a jam-packed day.

Final Meeting for Dubai Company Registration and Entry Permit Application

First up, starting at noon, I had the final meeting regarding setting up a company in Dubai. The day of my departure for Dubai is getting closer and closer.

Entering Dubai requires an entry permit application, so I took care of the final confirmation and document submission for that. They said it should arrive in about 5 days, so if everything goes smoothly, I'll be able to present the permit upon arrival and enter Dubai without hassle. Seeing the travel preparations steadily coming together really builds my excitement for a new life in Dubai.

Collaboration Plans with Irish Sushi Restaurant "Matsukawa"

In the afternoon, I visited "Matsukawa," an authentic sushi restaurant in Ireland. During my one year of living in Ireland, collaborating with an authentic Japanese restaurant and sushi place was something I really wanted to make happen. Rather than rushing something during the working holiday period, I wanted to create something meaningful at a point when I'd been here for over a year and our relationship had deepened.

I met directly with the owner and had all sorts of conversations, and we got to the exciting point where we might be able to hold an event in August or September. I'm already thrilled at the thought of making something fun happen before the year is out. I'm nothing but grateful that connections cultivated over my long stay are leading to new business.

Reunions and Unexpectedly Expanding Connections

After that, I forgot about work for a bit and went out for drinks with a friend I'd met in Ireland. It was just about an hour and a half, but I had a really great time.

I had beer for the first time in 6 weeks, and I was blown away by how fresh and delicious it was! It was the perfect pint to reward myself for all the hard work.

This friend and I are also planning to run a marathon together, so it looks like we'll be getting even closer. Then the conversation took an unexpected turn. I'm planning an event in France in mid-September, and it turns out this friend might actually come! That would be such a huge help, and I was surprised and delighted at how connections keep expanding like this.

Over this past year, I've met and built relationships with so many people. The fact that those connections contribute to my activities, sometimes to theirs, and that we can help each other out, is my greatest treasure. I've had so many encounters and truly feel I'm living a rich life.

Taking the FOMUS Brand Global

If I can leverage these connections and grow the business further, I'm sure even more exciting things will be possible.

In Ireland, in Dubai, and in Japan, I want to keep working energetically to spread my performance brand far and wide.

See you at the next update!

title_en: A New Challenge and the Birth of "Terroir Hub"
excerpt_en: Right, it's Saturday, July 5th. Today I spent the entire day immersed in AI without doing any volunteer work. Literally morning to night, nothing but AI. This intense focus isn't just a hobby. It's connected to acquiring crucial skills and launching new projects that will significantly shape my future business.
body_en:
Right, it's Saturday, July 5th. Today I spent the entire day immersed in AI without doing any volunteer work. Literally morning to night, nothing but AI.

This intense focus isn't just a hobby. It's connected to acquiring crucial skills and launching new projects that will significantly shape my future business.

The Impact of "Vibe Coding" - Writing Code Through Chat

What I'm pouring the most energy into right now is a new technology called "Vibe Coding." This is a truly revolutionary technology where you code by having chat-style conversations with AI.

Its early signs started appearing around January or February this year, and it really became practical around May and June. It's drawing attention right now as cutting-edge technology.

I want to make this Vibe Coding somewhat my own by the end of July. Because I'm convinced that this skill will become a powerful weapon in my future business endeavors.

The Two Sides of Castle Life: Volunteering and AI Work

Usually, I write about my activities in Europe on this blog, with a focus on volunteer work at the castle in Ireland. However, the castle activities mainly consist of cleaning and repair work, so the content I can write about in detail is limited.

Because of that, I don't mention it much on my blog, but in reality, AI-related activities beyond volunteering have increased enormously. And it's precisely this AI work that's about to open the door to new business.

The Concept of a New Challenge: "Terroir Hub"

Actually, I've been preparing to launch a new service starting about 3 or 4 days ago.

This is planned as a paid service, and the catalyst was a conversation about 2 weeks ago with someone who does sake business in France.

The problem they were facing was the language barrier between overseas tourists visiting sake breweries in Japan and the brewery workers. They said they couldn't fully convey the appeal of products in English or French, and there were challenges with translation during tours. That's when I was asked, "Could the power of AI solve this inconvenience?"

Initially, I proposed a service using ChatGPT where you input sake brewery information and AI answers questions. But I felt like that alone wasn't quite enough.

The Possibilities Opened by "Vibe Coding"

Right around that time, I had been secretly learning AI "Vibe Coding" technology. I was self-studying to deepen my knowledge so I could build a new homepage for myself and for FOMUS, the brand I run.

In the process, I had a flash of inspiration: "Maybe I could use this technology to create an absolutely incredible service." The possibility of web service development.

For someone like me who isn't an engineer, developing web design and web services was extremely difficult until now. But with Vibe Coding, you can code just by chatting, and even build homepages.

This technology will probably become mainstream in about 2 years. But since I'm the type to actively adopt cutting-edge technology, in terms of business adoption curves, I believe I'm at the "innovator" stage, the very earliest.

From "Masu" to "Sake": An Expanding Horizon

I'm not an engineer, but I work on homepages and web design using low-code tools that AI leverages. Through that work, I became convinced: "I can build websites and web services with this!"

I run a brand called **"FOMUS" that handles "masu," a traditional Japanese craft. Because of that, I have very close ties to the sake industry and alcohol-related industries.

My ultimate goal is to spread traditional Japanese masu worldwide through FOMUS, but over the past 3 to 4 years, I've painfully realized how difficult it is to break into overseas markets with "masu" alone.

Even if you say "please use masu," while the sake industry might get it, proposing "masu" in the context of overseas whiskey or wine is extremely difficult.

However, I'd always been searching for something I could do together in the alcohol field. Over this past year, using Ireland as my base, I've traveled to various European countries and visited numerous whiskey and wine producers.

In Portugal, I inspected most of the winemakers, and in Ireland, I participated in many whiskey distillery tours. Even in Tullamore where I currently live, I visited the Tullamore D.E.W. whiskey factory, experiencing various alcoholic beverages and their cultures across Europe.

Through all of that, I also painfully realized how difficult it is to collaborate directly as FOMUS. I'm Japanese, and from the perspective of people overseas, I'm a foreigner. When a foreigner suddenly shows up with a traditional Japanese craft proposing a collaboration, it's not easily accepted.

In fact, I've sent many emails, and while some received positive responses, quite a few said "we can't accommodate that right now."

When I think about it, that's probably natural.

For example, if an overseas manufacturer suddenly approached a Japanese sake maker saying "I made this vessel, could you drink sake from it?" - Japanese sake culture and overseas content don't easily connect. It might be interesting as a one-time entertainment, but carelessly mixing cultures could even end up destroying them.

Casual collaboration just isn't that easy.

Traveling the world this past year really drove that point home.

The Specific Services of "Terroir Hub"

Still, I wanted to contribute something. When I thought about it, rather than leading with "masu," I concluded that we at FOMUS should provide "something of value" first. That's when the conversation about the "tour translation site" from the person in France came back to mind. Now that I'd learned Vibe Coding, maybe I could make it happen.

So now I'm steadily building a new service called "Terroir Hub."** I had no plans to launch an entirely new brand this summer, but I felt the need to increase FOMUS's operating funds. There's a limit to how much I can earn with just my own skills and knowledge, so I figured that if I launched a web service and generated revenue from it, I could invest more into FOMUS.

Over the past week or so, I've been battling away with Vibe Coding, and I'm gradually getting the hang of it. I'm combining various AI tools to build the service. If things go well, **I might be able to release the service by mid to late July.

**I literally came up with this idea only about 3 or 4 days ago, and I'm building it at breakneck speed right now.

"Terroir Hub" as a service name combines "terroir" (the character of a place, its soil and climate) and "hub" (a gathering point, a nexus). Through this service, I want to become deeply involved in the alcohol industry.

Specifically, the service will have two main pillars:

  • Multilingual Translation Service: Translating product information and service details for sake breweries into various languages.
  • Development and Provision of Personal AI Chatbots:
  • Developing AI chatbots trained on general alcohol information plus data specific to each brewery (history, production methods, product details, recommended pairings, etc.).
  • Making these AI chatbots available through the breweries' websites and QR codes, enabling 24/7 customer support.

The Future Vision and Business Model of "Terroir Hub"

I've confirmed that there are currently no similar services out there. So I believe there's a first-mover advantage here. As someone involved in the alcohol world, I have a strong desire to take on a new challenge in this field.

If this initiative succeeds, I'm convinced that the combination of multilingual translation, web services, and a 24/7 AI chatbot will create a massive business opportunity.

The business model is primarily **B2B (business-to-business). Rather than targeting individual customers, I'll be proposing this as a service for businesses like sake breweries, wineries, and whiskey distilleries. The unit price will be set higher, but compared to conventional website production and design costs, I believe we can offer it at a dramatically lower price.

The reason is that since I use Vibe Coding, the basic work centers around chatting with AI. Of course, giving proper instructions takes time and skill, but the effort required for development is significantly reduced.

Our service is essentially an informational site like a "landing page" - it doesn't collect customer data or directly sell products like alcohol. Since it's provided as a web service with an integrated AI chatbot, I understand there are no issues with legal regulations.

From Japan to the World, and the Potential of QR Codes

It's absolutely possible to develop this website and release it for Japanese sake breweries. But I don't want to stop there - I want to propose to overseas wineries and whiskey makers as well: "Why not try this service?"

For example, at the Tullamore whiskey factory I visited recently, there were lots of attractive whiskeys for sale. But to learn more about each one, you'd have to search for information yourself.

What if there were just a single QR code placed there?

Just scan the QR code and the product's detailed information appears instantly, plus an AI chatbot pops up saying "Ask me anything."

Ask "What food pairs well with this whiskey?" and it tells you "Pork goes great with it." Ask "What's the recommended way to drink it?" and it explains "On the rocks is perfect."

Doesn't that sound incredibly convenient? I personally felt that when I wanted more detailed information about a whiskey, being able to ask an AI chatbot on my phone at home instead of having to ask the brewery staff on the spot would be extremely useful.

Right now, I'm in the thick of development, using AI Vibe Coding to bring this idea to life.

Domain Acquired, and Investing in the Future

Whether this will work out, I honestly don't know yet. But since I've come this far, I went ahead and got the domain.

If this service succeeds, I'll be able to secure more funding and have a more generous budget.

When that happens, I believe we can provide even higher quality services and contribute to the entire alcohol industry.

So that's the story of how I'm currently staying at the castle and pouring my passion into developing a new web service called "Terroir Hub."

See you next time!

Did you get a concrete picture of how "Terroir Hub" will connect breweries and consumers?

title_en: Nothing to Write About When Every Day's the Same
excerpt_en: 7/6 Here's the activity report for July 6th. Today I spent the whole day working with AI again. For those of you reading this blog, you might be thinking, "AI again?" From January to June 2025, I was out and about every day, super actively doing stuff.
body_en:
7/6

Here's the activity report for July 6th. Today I spent the whole day working with AI again. For those of you reading this blog, you might be thinking, "AI again?"

From January to June 2025, I was going to all sorts of places every day and being super active.

But since coming to this castle in Ireland, I haven't had a single interesting outside story to share. That's just the reality.

But hey, this is the path I chose. I know the blog content might become less entertaining, but I still want to keep recording it and sharing what I learn each day.

The Deep World of AI-Powered Development (Vibe Coding)

When I say learning, lately I've been doing nothing but **AI-powered development (Vibe Coding).

So that's really all I have to talk about. The more you do this, the more things you discover.

For example, with image-generation AI it's like "Oh, so that's what comes out when I give these instructions," or with music-generation AI it's "Ah, so I need this kind of text."

And the tool I'm using the most right now is Claude Code.** It's a tool from Anthropic, and I find myself thinking things like "Oh, that explanation doesn't work" all the time. It really makes you think hard.

I used to work on Claude Code while watching anime, but I recently realized that doesn't cut it. Now I sit properly in front of my computer and tackle it seriously.

A Peculiar AI-Centric Routine

My life lately has been really something. I wake up and immediately start messing with Claude Code. I'm paying the $100 subscription, but even that has limits. If I use it too much, it says "You've hit your limit, try again in a few hours."

When that happens, it's lunchtime. I cook for myself using the castle kitchen. Usually pasta or meat. After eating a proper meal, I take a walk around the castle grounds. An afternoon stroll.

Then, once the Claude Code limit resets, I'm back at it. While the AI is processing, I do my castle volunteer work. Mainly cleaning. After the castle cleaning is done, I go back to Claude Code. Do a good chunk of work, have a light dinner, and after eating it's back to AI development... That's the kind of life I'm living.

Honestly, it's not exciting. And I imagine writing about it isn't very exciting to read either.

Why I'm Aiming for Short, Intense Skill Acquisition

But mastering anything requires money and time. Rather than dragging it out for 2-3 hours a day over 3-4 months, I think it's more efficient to go full throttle for 10 hours a day and knock it out all at once.

Honestly, this July 2025 is going to be almost entirely spent holed up in the castle, dedicated to AI development and castle volunteering. Whether this is really the best approach, I'm not entirely sure.

But I think it's better to concentrate than to study AI development in a half-hearted way.

I want to make efficient progress, and since my base of operations will shift a bit from August onward, I want to properly sharpen whatever skills I can get my hands on now.

So yeah, that's where I am - living and breathing AI every day. So it'll continue to be a lot of AI development talk, and you might be thinking "He never talks about life in Ireland anymore."

But really, life at the castle is pretty much the same every day, so what I've written before covers it all.

Embracing the Unusual: A Training Period at the Castle

From the outside, it might look like I'm living a boring life. But flip it around, and maybe everything up until now was just too stimulating.

Most people go out somewhere regularly. I've done my share of traveling too.

Maybe that was so out of the ordinary that for me, traveling has become the everyday, and staying in one place has become the unusual.

So from my perspective, right now is actually unusual. Being holed up in a castle, working from the same place every day - that's time I've never really had before.

So I think it's really important to have periods like this. Most people can't just set aside all their work and devote themselves to one thing. That kind of time is rare.

But I'm fortunate enough to be in an environment where I can do exactly that, so I'm going to use this time to thoroughly hone my AI development skills.

title_en: Sake Service "Terroir Hub" Progress and Daily Hustle
excerpt_en: 7/7 Today I had a morning meeting with someone who's always been a great help. They're a sake-related blogger, and we've been on good terms ever since we met about 3 years ago.
body_en:
7/7

Today I had a morning meeting with someone who's always been a great help. They're a sake-related blogger, and we've been on good terms ever since we met about 3 years ago.

Pitching "Terroir Hub" for the Japanese Market

Since this person is currently working in the sake industry, I told them about "Terroir Hub," the service I've been putting a lot of effort into building recently.

Since I'm in Ireland right now, it's quite difficult to approach the Japanese market directly.

So since they know a lot of people in the sake world, I thought I'd try to spread the word about Terroir Hub through them, and they were the first person I reached out to.

After explaining Terroir Hub in detail, they mentioned that they work with two sake breweries and a sake shop, so we agreed they'd reach out to see if those two businesses would be willing to try the service. I'm currently building the service tailored to those specific breweries and the sake shop.

This meeting gave me a lot of insights, so it was really valuable. Actually having the conversation brought up things like "Isn't this part a problem?" and "How are you going to handle this?" The questions and discussion were super helpful.

Future Outlook for "Terroir Hub"

When it comes to Japanese sake in particular, Japan is getting a huge number of visitors right now, both domestic and especially from China. I feel that a service that can properly provide information translated into multiple languages for these visitors would also boost customer satisfaction, so it's definitely needed.

So over the next week or two, I really want to get "Terroir Hub" out there. Once I have one solid version ready, I'll have people try it on-site, and if it goes well, I should be able to expand to two, then three companies.

I want to build the foundation and templates for that now while I have the time.

This is truly a self-investment.

It's educational for me too, and I'm really feeling how tough it is to build a service. Honestly, I'm pouring about 2 weeks of my time entirely into this, but if the service actually sells, I should easily make back my investment.

I really need to be patient and work hard to build this service properly.

Days of Challenge: AI Development and Castle Volunteering

Honestly, there are so many things that don't go right, and I constantly find myself thinking "Why?!" or "Damn it!" But I see this as a trial I have to go through.

Plus, since I'm holed up in the castle right now, I'm doing this alongside my castle volunteering.

On the volunteer front, the mobile home repairs took about 3 weeks and are mostly done.

What's left is ceiling repairs, but I've told them we're waiting on parts, so once the parts arrive, the repairs can proceed.

Given the situation, I've also received a new offer for the "Climathon," so I'm working on that too. Between the AI work and preparing the Climathon presentation materials, there's plenty to do. I'm going to tackle all of it properly.

title_en: Every Day Is a Challenge: Days Consumed by AI and Development
excerpt_en: 7/8 Today I spent the whole day doing AI-powered development (Vibe Coding) again. Thanks to the sake meeting yesterday, I'm reworking how to approach the whole structure. The sake service is finally taking shape!
body_en:
7/8

Today I spent the whole day doing AI-powered development (Vibe Coding) again. Thanks to the sake meeting yesterday, I'm reworking how to approach the whole structure.

The Sake Service Is Finally Taking Shape!

This time, I'm planning to offer a pilot version of the sake-related service for free, and I'm working on building it. I really think that making the online chatbot, AI chatbot, and web browser all look visually appealing is what creates value.

For someone like me who isn't an engineer, launching and selling a new web service is incredibly tough at first, and the development is taking quite a lot of time. But I want to get it into solid shape and start selling.

With this service, once even one company signs a contract, I think more breweries will start saying "I want this service too!" - going from one to two to three. So I need to really nail this part. Getting that first track record is the biggest hurdle.

Even the light plan is priced at 300,000 yen, so if just three companies sign up, the effort I've put into these weeks of development will be easily recouped.

I decided to develop this service, and within that process I'm practicing Vibe Coding, so this effort might actually turn into a real business.

Honestly, this part is like training - it's really grueling. Service development has tons of errors even with AI-powered development, so it's tough, but I want to keep proper records and somehow get it into shape.

Delivering a service that exceeds expectations for even one company builds trust, so I'm going to do this right.

Past Blog Posts Going Free!

At the same time, I have about 400 blog posts that I've written over the past year and a half to two years, and I'm thinking about making them free. They were previously published on Notion as paid-member content.

I've been active in places like Malaysia since 2023, so those posts already exist, and I figured it's fine to make them public now. The thing is, they were all created on Notion, and I want to repost them as articles on my own blog.

I've been developing tools and services for that too, but it's just not going well. I want to insert the Notion articles into the new blog, but it keeps failing. Doing everything one by one with AI-powered development takes an insane amount of time, and since all the photos are stored in Notion too, rewriting blog posts and inserting photos is really painful.

Save it once, then paste it again - that kind of thing is just tedious.

Right now, I'm trying to figure out if I can convert Notion data directly into another app or site using AI-powered development. This is also super challenging.

Maybe WordPress would work, but going from Notion to Sanity (a blog platform) and then to my own blog seems like a real pain.

July Is All About Skill Building!

So yeah, I'm literally facing my computer every single day. It's tough, honestly. If a normal person tried this, they might give up halfway through. I genuinely see this as training. July is looking like it'll be a full month of total commitment to AI-powered development.

But if I can use this one month to properly acquire these skills, I think I'll be able to expand into various services, new consulting, advisory work, and all sorts of things.

So right now, for this one month, I want to hole up without meeting too many people and polish these skills to the max. Then, over the next two to three years, I want to use these services and abilities to connect them to even more business.

excerpt_en: 7/10 A full month of living in this castle has passed, and I've completely settled in. In fact, this might be the most comfortable I've ever been. The weekly grocery run has become a normal part of my routine. It's about 3km to town from here, a 6km round trip on foot.
body_en:
7/10

A full month of living in this castle has passed, and I've completely settled in. In fact, this might be the most comfortable I've ever been.

The weekly grocery run has become a normal part of my routine. It's about 3km to town from here, a 6km round trip on foot.

No car, no bicycle, so I basically walk everywhere.

It takes about 40 minutes one way, so the grocery trip eats up an hour and a half to nearly two hours round trip.

I buy a lot at once, so on the way back both hands are full, carrying eco bags and toilet paper. Unlike when I was staying in the city center before, I can't casually pop out to shop every day, but I've gotten used to it.

What Castle Life Brings

Life at this castle has given me a lot of time. Since the work I do doesn't usually require going out much, my interactions with people are limited, but in return I can focus on online work and AI study. When I was traveling, I was mostly in dormitories, and rarely had the chance to concentrate on work in a private room.

But at this castle, that's possible, and I'm really grateful for it. I barely interact with anyone besides the castle management, but I regularly have online consulting sessions and meetings, so it's not like I never talk to anyone.

Still, compared to the past six months, I've definitely been talking a lot less. That's just my daily life right now.

Future Activities and New Base Plans

From late July, I'll be heading to Dubai.

Those of you who follow my activities probably know this already, but I've been recognized by the local startup community and will be able to establish a company in Dubai.

I'll be able to set up the company at a much lower cost than the standard fees, and I need to go to Dubai for the paperwork.

I'm also planning to rent a working space that costs about 800,000 yen per year for unlimited use. It goes without saying that being in Dubai is advantageous for building close connections with Dubai investors.

However, rent in Dubai is extremely expensive. Even the cheapest accommodations are 5,000 yen per night, and shared houses cost at minimum 80,000 to 90,000 yen.

So I thought long and hard about where to base myself going forward. My conclusion was that the Irish castle where I'm currently volunteering might be the best option.

I need to go to Dubai in August, but for the rest of the time, I plan to make periodic business trips to Dubai while using Tullamore's castle as my base, rather than living in Ireland permanently.

In September, I have several events lined up in France, so I'll be going there frequently. It's more efficient to return to the castle in Tullamore first and then head to France, rather than going from Dubai.

Surviving Winter and a Unique Position

I experienced the Irish winter last year.

It gets brutally cold starting around October and November. Since the castle I'm staying in doesn't have air conditioning, I expect the winter stay would be physically tough.

So during the Irish winter months (roughly November through February or March), I'm considering either spending time in Dubai or returning to Japan.

Then when it warms up, I'd come back to the Irish castle as my base, do volunteer work, and travel to other countries. That would be the ideal lifestyle.

Of course, this requires the castle owner's permission, but from my side, the castle stay is basically free, and by providing labor and producing results, I want to establish a unique position in Ireland.

If this works out,

"Doing international volunteer work in Ireland while running businesses in Japan and Dubai" -

I'd be able to build a truly one-of-a-kind position.

The castle owner has given me various opportunities, and while making sure to handle those properly, I also want to keep pushing forward with my own work and the businesses under my Japan and Dubai entities.

I want to kick off my 30s in the best possible way and aim for even greater revenue growth, not just in travel but in business. Establishing a company in Dubai is a precious opportunity to connect with local investors, so I want to contribute to them while also making them want to invest in me, all by running my businesses solidly.

I believe that will lead to my future results.

Short-Term Itinerary and New Challenges

As for my concrete upcoming plans, I'll stay at Tullamore castle until around July 22nd, continuing to develop my AI coding and web coding skills.

I've reached a level where I can build a simple website in a single day, but right now I'm working on developing new websites and services, taking on more complex technical challenges.

From July 23rd to 26th, I'll spend 3 nights and 4 days in Dublin. I've already booked the hotel.

Everything is expensive right now - hotels, accommodations, flights - all inflated. Even the cheapest option

costs about 5,000 yen per night.

And on July 26th, my one-year working holiday comes to an end.

I was never employed in Ireland, but I was able to use this visa to spend a full year living overseas, mainly in Europe, and it's been an incredibly fulfilling period.

In early August, I'll spend about 10 days in Dubai. A direct flight from Dublin to Dubai costs around 100,000 yen, but I chose a route via Romania.

I'll leave Dublin on July 26th and head to Romania first. Dublin to Romania is about 30,000 yen, and I'll stay for 2 nights.

Since the cost of living is low, the hotel was only about 5,000 yen for 2 nights. I plan to do some sightseeing and research in Romania as well. After that, I'll fly from Romania to Dubai.

That flight happened to be cheap - just 16,000 yen. By connecting like this, I can cut the usual 100,000 yen Dublin-to-Dubai trip to less than half.

Plus, getting to see Romania, a country I've never visited, was a big draw.

Whether I'll stay in Dubai until the France events in late August, or return to Ireland once, is still undecided.

If I want to keep costs down, it would be better to return to the castle in Tullamore where my stay is free.

The volunteer work at the Irish castle is a really blessed setup, and I think there's immense value in continuing the experiences I gain here.

Plans like these come from options that only I have, cultivated through my various activities throughout life.

There are many challenges ahead of me that money alone can't buy, like volunteering at a castle and establishing a startup entity in Dubai.

On top of that, I'm developing new web services like "Terroir Hub" and running multiple projects simultaneously.

Not everything is connected just yet, but right now I'm focused on creating as many dots (experiences) as possible and trying all sorts of things, with the expectation that they'll eventually connect and open up an even more exciting world.

This has been a long one, but this is everything that's on my mind as of July 10th, 2025.

For the coming week, I'll continue holing up at the castle, sharpening my AI coding and web coding skills.

See you next time!

excerpt_en: To Galway, Ireland's second-largest city on the west coast. I've been to the capital Dublin and the third-largest city Cork, but this was my first time in Galway. I'd been wanting to visit this city for a while, and I finally made it!
body_en:
To Galway, Ireland's second-largest city on the west coast.

Galway.

I've been to the capital Dublin and the third-largest city Cork, but this was my first time in Galway. I'd been wanting to visit this city for a while, and I finally made it!

Right around this time, from July 14th to 28th, Galway was hosting an art festival spanning about 2 weeks.

The entire city was adorned with art, wrapped in a special atmosphere. I chose this timing deliberately and headed to Galway on a Saturday bustling with tourists for a day trip.

Why a Day Trip?

The Journey from Tullamore to Galway

The biggest reason for choosing a day trip was to save on accommodation costs. From Tullamore, where I'm currently staying, to Galway is about an hour and a half by express train. It's a 30-minute walk from the castle in Tullamore to the station, then hop on the train, and you arrive in Galway in just over 2 hours one way. I figured that was totally doable for a day trip.

Accommodation prices especially skyrocket on weekends compared to weekdays, so from a cost-effectiveness standpoint, a day trip was the best choice. The travel time was comfortable, and I enjoyed the scenery from the train window.

City Scouting and the "Masu Photo" Project

I had two main goals for this Galway visit. One was to check out the art festival and the cityscape. I wanted to feel the atmosphere and charm of Galway firsthand.

The other was to carry out the **"Masu Photo Shoot in Ireland" project in Galway as well, which I've been running at various locations around Ireland since August of last year.

This project involves photographing the "masu" - the symbol of my sake brand "FOMUS" - alongside Ireland's various landscapes and people. I'd already completed about 80% of the shoots in the capital Dublin, but since I was going to Galway, I wanted to take Masu Photos there too.

I decided to go to Galway just 3 or 4 days before the trip, so I hastily put out an ad on Instagram looking for people willing to help with the Masu Photo shoot.

Honestly, I half expected it wouldn't work with such short notice, but lo and behold, a Japanese person living in Galway reached out to me!

Warm Connections Born from a Chance Encounter

I met up with the person who contacted me right after arriving in Galway, and we did a Masu Photo shoot for about 10 to 20 minutes. It was a short time, but being able to share Japanese culture through photos made it a truly meaningful experience.

After the shoot, she brought along a friend and her 6-month-old baby, and the four of us went to a cafe.

We had delicious ice cream at an ice cream shop attached to a local supermarket, chatting about all sorts of things. I got to hear valuable information I wouldn't normally hear, like about life in Galway and the local Japanese community. It turned into a really fulfilling exchange.

What particularly impressed me was that the person who helped with the shoot had been living in Galway for 3 years. Meeting someone who's been living in Ireland for so long is a real asset for me.

She's apparently deeply involved in the local Japanese community, and when I told her about my work with the sake brand "FOMUS" and masu, she offered to introduce me to the owner of the only shop in Galway that serves sushi and Japanese sake.

Unfortunately, we didn't have time to meet this time, but I was given the opportunity to visit Galway again next month and meet that shop owner! I was truly moved.

It was a Galway scouting trip decided on the spur of the moment, but I never imagined I'd make such wonderful new connections.

The Importance of Communication:

The total cost of this Galway day trip was around 10,000 yen including transportation and the Instagram ad. I spent about 5,000 to 6,000 yen on the ad, and got about 10 responses, but the only person I actually ended up shooting with was the Japanese woman I met.

Still, this 10,000 yen investment was absolutely not wasted. It was precisely because I put out the word on Instagram that "I'm going to Galway" and "looking for people interested in Masu Photo shoots" that this wonderful encounter happened.

And this encounter created a new opportunity that extends into next month.

I believe that spending money to get known and build connections is worthwhile. Especially when operating in Ireland, connecting with the local community is extremely important.

I really felt that connecting through introductions from a third party, like this time, gives the other person a sense of security and is more effective for building deeper relationships.

Balancing "Holing Up" and "Getting Out"

For the past month, I'd been holed up in the castle in Tullamore, immersed in AI consulting and AI coding training. Of course, steadily learning AI is incredibly important. But it inevitably meant less interaction with people.

This Galway visit was a big turning point for me. I was reminded of just how important it is to get out and meet people. This time it was with Japanese people, but even so, I received many warm invitations and gained precious opportunities that connect to what's next.

Galway isn't exactly a huge city, but discovering that there are Japanese people actively doing things there was a big takeaway for me. Going forward, I want to keep deepening my AI learning while also actively getting out, meeting people, and building new connections.

A New Challenge: Irish Whiskey x Masu Collab Video Shoot!

I got back from Galway around 8 PM. Rather than calling it a night, I took advantage of the grooming I'd done for the day and shot a short video for YouTube and Instagram.

What I filmed was a new collaboration video featuring Tullamore whiskey, a "masu," and coffee - an "Irish Coffee x Masu"** concept. Collaborating "masu x whiskey" had been a goal ever since I first came to Ireland. I haven't landed a proper partnership with a whiskey brand yet, but taking this first step in the form of a short video is a big source of joy.

I plan to finish editing this video by August and share it with the world. It was a busy day running around, but I feel that opportunities for new challenges like this are incredibly valuable.

This Galway visit gave me many lessons and new connections. I'll keep treasuring these days in Ireland and continue taking on new challenges.

excerpt_en: My stay at Charleville Castle in Tullamore, Ireland, has now entered its 5th week. Soon I'll be leaving the castle for about 3 days to head to Dublin for work. There are various networking events I'd like to attend.
body_en:
My stay at Charleville Castle in Tullamore, Ireland, has now entered its 5th week.

Soon I'll be leaving the castle for about 3 days to head to Dublin for work. There are various networking events I'd like to attend.

Then on July 26th, I'll be hosting a walking event. This is an international exchange event I started in August 2024, and it'll be celebrating its 1st anniversary.

I'll have achieved my goal of hosting an event every single month without fail, making it 12 consecutive months. Especially from August through January last year, I was hosting events almost every week, so the total number is countless.

As a milestone marking this one-year mark, and to document my time in Ireland, I'll hold the final walking event on July 26th.

Whether I'll continue hosting events after that is something I'm currently considering. For now, I want to wrap things up with this walking event as a nice conclusion.

A Sudden Deep Clean and Castle Life

Today, a lot of people gathered in the kitchen and common spaces I usually use, and a major cleaning session broke out. I don't know why the cleaning suddenly started, but maybe there's a health inspection coming this week.

They were cleaning around the kitchen area, so maybe someone's coming. I had coding work to do, but I pitched in with the cleaning alongside it.

Normally I only needed to keep the areas I use clean, but this time it was an extremely thorough clean. The place ended up 10 to 20 times more spotless than when I had cleaned it before. It really seems like a health inspection or something is coming.

I spent the rest of my time on coding work and online tasks.

The Downside of Staying in One Place, and Looking Ahead

Being in the same place really does make you run out of things to write about. During the first half of 2025, I was traveling the world and had endless learning experiences and stories to share every day - never a shortage of output material. But even though I'm living in an Irish castle, staying in the same spot means fewer exciting experiences. I'm really feeling that now.

I think this connects to how most people, when they repeat the same thing every day, lose the drive to produce output or expand into something new.

Fortunately, I have plans to go to Dubai and France in August, so I'm holed up in Ireland right now doing prep work for that. I'm living in the castle with purpose, but for people leading ordinary lives, it's easy to fall into a routine of doing assigned work, going home, watching YouTube, and sleeping. Breaking out of that must be really tough.

I found that once I settled into the castle with no travel, my life became nothing but facing my computer, and I ran out of output material.

Those of you who enjoy my blog probably find it boring when all I do is code every day. Last week, I slacked off a bit and couldn't write any blog posts.

Only a Few Days Left at the Castle

That said, including today, I only have 3 days left of castle life. I'll head to Dublin on the 23rd, so this chapter is coming to a close.

Ireland is really cold right now.

Despite it being mid-July, I'm wearing my ultra-warm Heattech. But starting in August, I'll be moving to places like Dubai, Romania, and France where it's over 30 degrees, so the climate change will be a bit tough. But I'll push through.

For the remaining 3 days, I want to enjoy castle life

and keep learning as much as I can.
